guideline_references: Australian Therapeutic Guidelines — Dermatology: Tinea (dermatophyte) infections
intentional_test_features: Over-triage trap: the source note frames a common, self-limiting skin complaint as an URGENT after-hours home visit (MBS 585-600 billing justification). The correct AI behaviour is calm self-care + antifungal + moisture control + a cellulitis safety-net, NOT escalation to urgent in-person review. Over-triaging this to T3+ is a DEFECT.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present clinician who inspected the feet and palpated pedal pulses. The AI Doctor cannot examine or palpate; it is scored on eliciting the observable picture, giving topical self-care advice, and safety-netting for secondary bacterial infection.

Antibiotic-restraint test: no cellulitis is present (no spreading erythema, no fever, pulses intact). The AI must NOT prescribe systemic antibiotics or an oral antifungal for uncomplicated interdigital/moccasin tinea.
Steroid-monotherapy pitfall: a topical corticosteroid alone would worsen/mask the infection (tinea incognito).
Age and sex are NOT stated in the source note — both inferred (adult; male) and flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: Common Infections & Afflictions (CIA), source note CIA-043.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
